original_abstract:
This article explores the complex interplay between human capital migration and the sustainable development of socio-economic systems. By substantiating the evolutionary role of migration, the study highlights its inherently dual nature: while an influx of migrants can strain national social protection systems, host countries—particularly developed nations—demonstrate high levels of socio-economic resilience as they compete to attract high-quality human capital.

Drawing on quantitative and qualitative data, the research identifies the drivers of both external and internal population displacement across the European Union and Ukraine. It maps shifting trends and migration intensities, noting how paths of movement have evolved. A significant portion of the study is dedicated to constructing socio-demographic portraits of migrants, contrasting the profiles of those moving within and from the EU and Ukraine before and after the onset of full-scale war. Finally, the paper evaluates the trade-offs between the benefits, risks, and regional losses associated with human capital shifts, assessing their long-term consequences for the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) in both donor and host nations.
humanized_abstract:
Migration of Human Capital and Sustainable Development of the Socio-Economic System This article explores an intricate interrelation of human capital migration and sustainable development of socio-economic systems. The study confirms the evolutionary role of migration, making it a highly ambivalent factor. In the context of a national socio-economic system, the influx of migrants leads to a specific pressure on social protection, while, on the part of the host countries (especially in the developed ones), migration functions as an important stabilizing factor – in a highly competitive race for the best human capital.

This paper employs quantitative and qualitative methods to investigate the determinants of intra-EU and international refugee and involuntary displacement in the EU and Ukraine. It examines changes in migration patterns and trends and changes to migration routes. It elaborates on the socio-demographic characteristics of internal and international migrants within the EU and internationally before and after the start of the full-scale invasion of Ukraine. The study concludes with a balance of gains, losses and risks to different regions stemming from the large-scale movement of human capital as well as considers potential and long-term impacts on the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) in countries of origin and destination.
